title: On-Chip Quantum Randomness Amplification
authors: Lang Li, Yutian Wu, Giulio Chiribella, Ravishankar Ramanathan
abstract: Randomness amplification, the task of extracting uniform private bits from biased seeds that may be partly known by a malicious third party, is of central importance in cryptography. The highest security in this task is provided by a class of quantum protocols known as device-independent, which however are challenging to integrate into scalable devices. Semi-device-independent (SDI) protocols are a promising alternative that guarantees security under few natural assumptions, such as bounds on the amount of energy used by the devices. Here, we provide the first demonstration of SDI randomness amplification on an integrated silicon photonic chip, achieving a throughput rate of 20 Mbps suitable for practical applications. This rate is achieved through a novel technique for SDI entropy certification, which delivers strictly tighter von Neumann entropy bounds compared to existing methods and remains valid even if the preparation and measurement devices share quantum correlations. Overall, the methods developed in this work enable the integration of SDI technology into portable telecom devices, opening up a new generation of quantum cryptographic hardware.
summary: The paper presents a breakthrough demonstration of on-chip Quantum Randomness Amplification using Semi-Device-Independent protocols. By integrating advanced variational entropy certification techniques onto a silicon photonic chip, the authors achieved a high, practical throughput rate of 20 Mbps. This work paves the way for deploying quantum cryptographic hardware in portable telecom devices.
main_problem: The central problem is achieving high-rate, secure randomness amplification using Semi-Device-Independent (SDI) protocols.
main_result: The authors demonstrated the first on-chip implementation of SDI randomness amplification on a silicon photonic chip, achieving a practical throughput rate of 20 Mbps.
method: They combine advanced theoretical techniques—specifically an upgraded variational Gauss-Radau method for entropy certification—with state-of-the-art integrated photonics hardware.
model_or_system: The system is an integrated silicon photonic chip utilizing an electro-optical (EO) and opto-electrical (OE) platform operating at 1550 nm. It implements a protocol that extracts uniform bits from biased quantum seeds generated via an $\epsilon$-biased Santha-Vazirani source.
key_observables: Certified von Neumann entropy, MDL Score, throughput rate (20 Mbps), and frequency responses ($\approx 47 	ext{ GHz}$ EO, $52 	ext{ GHz}$ OE).
important_parameters: Bias parameter $\epsilon$ (e.g., 0.12); energy bound $\omega$; raw data length ($n=10^{11}$ bits); output security level ($\epsilon_{	ext{sec}} \le 10^{-12}$).
main_assumptions: The protocol relies on low-energy constraints ($\omega$) and the assumption that variational methods provide tighter entropy bounds than standard Gauss-Radau techniques.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate experimental setups, frequency responses (EO/OE), and comparative plots showing certified entropy lower bounds derived from different theoretical methods versus the source bias $\epsilon$.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the need for SDI protocols, then introducing the variational Gauss-Radau method to tighten entropy bounds, followed by detailed characterization of the integrated photonic hardware (EO/OE responses), and culminating in the demonstration of high-rate randomness generation.
why_interesting: This work directly bridges fundamental quantum information theory (entropy bounding) with cutting-edge solid-state photonics engineering, providing a concrete blueprint for next-generation quantum cryptographic hardware.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Schematic of our energy-constrained SDI randomness amplification protocol. A sequence of imperfect random bits is provided by an ε-biased Santha-Vazirani source (left). The sequence is partitioned into a subsequence X, used as input for an untrusted device, and another subsequence Z, used as a seed for a randomness extractor. The untrusted device consists of a preparation module (P), which prepares states with energy upper bounded by a given value ω, and a measurement module (M), which produces a string of classical output bits B. The preparation and measurement module, instead, are allowed to be correlated, by sharing an entangled state σP M (dashed line ), which may be correlated...

FIG. 2: Certified randomness in the presence of classical correlations between preparation and mea- surement devices. The plots display certified single-round entropy bounds as a function of the Santha-Vazirani source bias ε ∈[0, 0.5]. The blue curve represents the standard conditional min-entropy H(ε,ω) min (B|X, E, Λ). The orange curve depicts the conditional von Neumann entropy H(ε,ω)(B|X, E, Λ) computed via standard Gauss-Radau method, while the green dashed curve shows the improved bound using our variational method. Subplots (a)–(d), with suitable choices of parameters in range: (a) ω(ε) ≤0.068, (b) ω(ε) ≤0.065, (c) ω(ε) ≤0.064, (d) ω(ε) ≤0.060, correspond to varying energy...

FIG. 3: Experimental SDI randomness amplification. (a) Schematics of the setup. The light emitted by 1550 nm laser is attenuated by a variable optical attenuator (VOA). The attenuated light then enters into an integrated electronic/optical (E/O) platform, which produces the coherent states used by our protocol. The coherent states exit the E/O platform through the optical output port (upper link in the picture) and undergo a test to verify that they satify the required energy bound. They go through a 90:10 beam splitter (BS), and the smaller fraction of the light is directed to a power meter (top right), while the larger fraction eventually undergoes a heterodyne measurement. Before...

FIG. 4: Experimentally certified entropies. The three curves show three lower bounds on the von Neu- mann entropy as a function of the SV source bias under the experimental parameters of our setup. Specifically, the bounds are obtained from our variational Gauss- Radau method (green dashed line), from the standard node Gauss-Radau method (orange solid line), and from the min-entropy baseline (blue solid line). In both panels, the insets provide a magnified view of the low-entropy re- gion ε ∈[0.11, 0.13]. The star markers show the certified values for the representative value ε = 0.12.

FIG. 1: Randomness Amplification Protocol. Our protocol consists of three stages. The first stage is the generation of quantum randomness through a prepare-and-measure setup, in which the prepared states have bounded energy, close to the ground state energy. The second stage is the estimation of the MDL score from the empirical data. If the MDL score is below a given threshold, the protocol proceeds to the third and final state: extraction of randomness from the measurement outcomes B and from an additional bit string Z generated by the SV source.
